name: Karna Full
description: Karna, also known as Vasusena, Angaraja, and Radheya, is a central figure in the Hindu epic Mahabharata. He was the son of the sun god Surya and Kunti, princess of the Kunti kingdom, and thus an elder half-brother of the Pandavas. Abandoned at birth, he was raised by a charioteer and his wife, leading a life of hardship and social stigma despite his innate nobility and martial prowess. Known for his unwavering loyalty, generosity, and exceptional archery skills, Karna became a close friend and ally of Duryodhana, the eldest Kaurava, and fought on their side in the Kurukshetra War. His tragic fate is largely determined by the circumstances of his birth and the curses he incurred, including one that caused him to forget vital mantras for invoking divine weapons at his moment of need. He was a formidable warrior, rivaling Arjuna in skill, and his death at the hands of Arjuna is one of the most poignant and debated events in the epic, highlighting themes of dharma, fate, and social injustice. Karna's character is often portrayed as a symbol of righteousness and selflessness despite being on the 'wrong' side, showcasing complex moral dilemmas and the consequences of societal prejudices. He is revered for his दानवीर (daanaveer) nature, his willingness to give even his life for a promise.
subtitle: The Tragic Hero of the Mahabharata

keyMyths.title: Karna's Birth and Abandonment
keyMyths.description: Kunti, as a young maiden, received a boon to invoke any god and beget a child. Out of curiosity, she invoked Surya, resulting in Karna's birth, but fearing social stigma, she abandoned him in a river.

keyMyths.title: Karna's Kavacha and Kundala
keyMyths.description: Karna was born with impenetrable armor (Kavacha) and earrings (Kundala), making him invincible. Indra, Arjuna's father, disguised himself as a beggar and tricked Karna into donating them, weakening him significantly.

keyMyths.title: Karna's Curse by Parashurama
keyMyths.description: Karna learned archery from Parashurama by pretending to be a Brahmin. When Parashurama discovered Karna's true Kshatriya identity, he cursed Karna to forget the mantras of the Brahmastra at his moment of need.

sources.author: Vyasa
